You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE resets player to start position. Player confined to top corridor (can only move LEFT/RIGHT). UP and DOWN are blocked at the rightmost position. Need to find where DOWN passages exist.
- Goal model: Navigate from a start point (likely the blue area at top-left) to an end point (likely the blue area at bottom-right), possibly following the red path.
- Action model: Directional keys move the player through the maze when passages exist. Movement is 6-cell jumps. Blocked directions don't move the player but still consume a step (timer shrinks).
- Open questions: What does SPACE do? How does the player navigate through the maze?
- Plan: Go RIGHT once from start, then try DOWN to find a vertical passage.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.content:
Now I see the full picture! Let me re-analyze:
- id=6: color=b (blue), pixels=24, boundary=[[8, 20], [8, 24], [12, 24], [12, 20]] - This is the PLAYER! A 4x4 blue square at rows 8-12, cols 20-24.
- id=7: color=R (red), pixels=82 - This is the RED PATH! It's not the player.
- id=5: color=B (black), pixels=879 - This is the BLACK MAZE structure.
- id=11: color=b (blue), pixels=19 - This is the TARGET at bottom-right (rows 49-55, cols 43-49).
So the player is id=6 (blue, 4x4), currently at rows 8-12, cols 20-24. The red path (id=7) is a separate object.
The player moved RIGHT from start (cols 14-18) to cols 20-24. DOWN was blocked.
Now I need to understand the maze structure better. The black maze (id=5) has the path boundary I saw earlier. The player moves through the black corridors.
Looking at the image, the player is in the top corridor. The red path (id=7) runs along the right side of the maze. The target (id=11) is at bottom-right.
The player needs to navigate from the top-left to the bottom-right. The red path seems to indicate the solution route.
Let me look at the red path boundary more carefully:
[[9, 39], [9, 41], [11, 41], [12, 40], [40, 40], [40, 19], [41, 18], [42, 18], [42, 14], [41, 15], [40, 14], [39, 15], [38, 14], [38, 18], [39, 18], [40, 19], [40, 39], [39, 40], [12, 40], [11, 39]]
This traces:
- [9, 39] to [12, 40]: top-right area
- [12, 40] to [40, 40]: down the right side
- [40, 40] to [40, 19]: left along bottom
- [40, 19] to [42, 14]: down-left corner
- [42, 14] to [38, 14]: up the left side
- [38, 14] to [40, 19]: right along middle
- [40, 19] to [40, 39]: right along bottom
- [40, 39] to [12, 40]: up the right side
Wait, this is confusing. The red path seems to trace a complex route. Let me think about this differently.
Actually, I think the red path is the "solution" path that the player needs to follow. The player is currently at the top corridor (rows 8-12, cols 20-24). The red path starts at the top-right and goes down.
But the player can't go DOWN from the top corridor. Maybe the player needs to reach a specific position where DOWN is possible.
Let me look at the maze structure. The black maze (id=5) has a boundary that traces the outer perimeter. The white areas (id=9, id=10) are chambers inside the maze.
The player is in the black corridor. The white chambers are "holes" in the maze. The player moves through the black corridors.
Looking at the image:
- Top corridor: rows 8-12, from col 14 to col 42 (approximately)
- The player is at cols 20-24
- The red path is at the right side, starting around row 9
Maybe the player needs to move RIGHT to align with the red path, then something happens.
Actually, let me reconsider the game mechanics. The player is a 4x4 blue square. The target is a 4x4 blue square at bottom-right. The red path connects the top-right to the bottom-right.
Maybe the game is:
1. Move the player along the top corridor to the rightmost position
2. Press SPACE to "enter" the red path
3. The player then follows the red path to the target
But I already tried this - SPACE just resets the player to start.
Wait, maybe SPACE does different things depending on the player's position. When I pressed SPACE at the start position, it revealed the path. When I pressed SPACE at the rightmost position, it reset the player.
Let me try a different approach. Let me look at the red path more carefully. The red path (id=7) has 82 pixels and its boundary traces a route. Maybe the red path is the "active" path that the player needs to trace.
Actually, I just realized something. The red path boundary includes points like [40, 40], [40, 19], [42, 14], etc. These are in the lower part of the maze. The player is at the top. How does the player reach the lower part?
Let me look at the white chambers. id=9 is at rows 14-18, cols 20-24 (a 4x4 white square). id=10 is a larger white area at rows 14-38, cols 20-38.
The player is at rows 8-12, cols 20-24. Below the player is the white chamber at rows 14-18, cols 20-24. Maybe the player can move DOWN into this chamber?
But DOWN was blocked. Let me try moving RIGHT more to reach a position where DOWN is possible.
Actually, wait. Let me re-examine the maze. The player is at rows 8-12, cols 20-24. The white chamber (id=9) is at rows 14-18, cols 20-24. There's a 2-row gap between the player and the chamber (rows 12-14).
The player is 4 rows tall (rows 8-12). If the player moves DOWN, it would move to rows 14-18. But the white chamber is also at rows 14-18. So maybe the player CAN move DOWN into the white chamber?
But DOWN was blocked. Let me check if the player is actually at the right position. The player boundary is [[8, 20], [8, 24], [12, 24], [12, 20]], which is rows 8-12, cols 20-24.
Hmm, but the white chamber is at rows 14-18, cols 20-24. If the player moves DOWN by 6 rows, it would go to rows 14-18. But there's a 2-row gap (rows 12-14) which is part of the black maze.
Wait, the player is 4 rows tall. If it moves DOWN by 6 rows, the new position would be rows 14-18. But the white chamber is also 4 rows tall at rows 14-18. So the player would overlap with the white chamber.
Maybe the player can't move DOWN because it would overlap with the white chamber? Or maybe the player needs to be at a different column to move DOWN?
Let me try moving RIGHT again and then DOWN.
